narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 41% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your profile, including your second-decile ranking in both first-year and third-year school grades—well below the typical student's fifth- or sixth-decile standing—along with your LSAT score of 28, which is notably lower than the average of around 37. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 is just slightly under the usual 3.2, while your middle family income bracket aligns more closely with peers, and as a full-time female Asian student, your demographics are represented but less common than white males in the applicant pool. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your third-year decile ranking, which had the biggest push toward predicting a higher risk of bar failure, followed closely by your LSAT score and then your first-year decile. These top three factors carried more weight than others like your undergraduate GPA, family income, racial background, or gender, all of which played smaller roles but still nudged the prediction slightly toward risk. Compared to typical students who get approved, your grades and LSAT stand out as weaker signals of bar exam readiness, even though your plan to study full-time is standard. While no single factor dooms an application, this combination tipped the scales to a concerning probability. The good news is you can take actionable steps to strengthen your profile for future applications or bar prep. Focus on boosting your academic deciles through better study habits or extra coursework, as school performance is something you can directly improve over time. Retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average could have a major impact, given its high influence here. Building a stronger undergraduate record, if possible through additional classes or a post-bac program, might also help offset these concerns. With targeted effort on these changeable areas, you could shift future predictions in your favor and demonstrate your potential to succeed on the bar exam. Keep pushing forward—you've got this.
